title: Print & Output
description: Learn how to display text and data in Python using the print() function.

steps.title: What is print()?
steps.content:
What is print()?

In Python, print() is a built-in function that displays output to the screen. It's one of the first things every Python programmer learns.

Whenever you want to show something — a message, a number, or a result — you use print().

print("Hello, World!")

Running the code above would display:

Hello, World!

Use the editor below to run it yourself. Click Run and see the output appear.
steps.js: PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: 'editor-intro', initialCode: 'print("Hello, World!")', label: 'Try it out' });

steps.title: Printing Text
steps.content:
Printing Text (Strings)

Text in Python is called a string. Strings must be wrapped in quotes — either single ' or double " quotes.

Tip: Use double quotes when your text contains an apostrophe, so Python doesn't get confused.

Try editing the code below and running it:

steps.title: Printing Numbers
steps.content:
Printing Numbers

Numbers don't need quotes. You can print integers and floats directly, and Python will evaluate expressions before printing.

Note: 100 + 200 prints 300 — Python does the maths first.
steps.js:
PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: 'editor-numbers', initialCode: 'print(42)
print(3.14)
print(100 + 200)
print(10 * 5)', label: 'Numbers' });

steps.title: Multiple Values
steps.content:
Printing Multiple Values

Separate items with commas — Python adds a space between each one automatically.

steps.title: sep and end
steps.content:
Customising with sep and end

sep controls what goes between items. end controls what's printed at the end of the line (default is a newline).
steps.js:
PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: 'editor-sep', initialCode: 'print("2024", "01", "15", sep="-")
print("a", "b", "c", sep=", ")
print("Hello", end=" ")
print("World!")', label: 'sep and end' });

steps.title: f-Strings
steps.content:
f-Strings

Put f before the opening quote, then use {} to embed variables or expressions directly in the string.
steps.js:
PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: 'editor-fstr', initialCode: 'name = "Alice"
age = 25
print(f"My name is {name} and I am {age} years old.")

width = 5
height = 3
print(f"The area is {width * height} square metres.")', label: 'f-Strings' });

steps.title: Challenge: Hello
steps.content:
Challenge 1 — Say Hello

Write a line of Python that prints exactly:

Hello, Python!

Your output must match exactly, including the comma and exclamation mark. Click Run to check.

steps.title: Challenge: Calculate
steps.content:
Challenge 2 — Calculate and Print

Use print() to display the result of 17 multiplied by 6. Don't type the answer directly — let Python calculate it.

102

Hint: the multiplication operator in Python is *.

steps.title: Challenge: f-String
steps.content:
Challenge 3 — Build an f-String

Two variables are already defined. Use an f-string to print:

My favourite language is Python and it is 34 years old.

You must use an f-string (not string concatenation). The numbers must come from the variables.

steps.title: Quick Quiz
steps.content:
Quick Quiz

Four questions — select the correct answer for each, then click Submit.
steps.css: #quiz-container .q-block { margin-bottom: 1.5rem; } #quiz-container .q-block p { font-weight: 600; margin-bottom: 0.5rem; color: #374151; } #quiz-container .option { display: block; width: 100%; text-align: left; padding: 0.5rem 0.75rem; margin-bottom: 0.4rem; border: 1px solid #d1d5db; border-radius: 0.375rem; cursor: pointer; background: #f9fafb; font-size: 0.9rem; transition: background 0.15s; } #quiz-container .option:hover:not(:disabled) { background: #e5e7eb; } #quiz-container .option.correct { background: #d1fae5; border-color: #10b981; color: #065f46; } #quiz-container .option.wrong { background: #fee2e2; border-color: #ef4444; color: #991b1b; } #quiz-submit { margin-top: 1rem; padding: 0.4rem 1.5rem; background: #2563eb; color: white; border-radius: 0.375rem; cursor: pointer; border: none; font-size: 0.9rem; }
